title: Open Field Behavior in Mice
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"cj5uo","text":"The open field test is widely used to assess spontaneous locomotor activity, exploratory behavior, and anxiety-like responses in rodents. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"d5uim","text":"Wear appropriate PPE as required by your institution. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"9tmoq","text":"Open field arena: 50 x 50 x 50 cm chambers","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"iiv4","text":"Video recording: A camera mounted on the ceiling above the chambers, adjusted to ensure full coverage of the arena. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Place the mouse gently in the center of the open field arena.
The test can last between 15 and 60 minutes, depending on the experimental design.
After allotted time, stop the recording and return the mouse to its home cage.
Optional observation: Record the number of fecal boli and urine spots as additional indicators of anxiety.
Thoroughly clean the chambers with 70% ethanol between trials and after testing to eliminate odor cues from previous animals.
creator: Christine Weber-Schmidt
